title: Add nav bar to dashboard
analysis:
The dashboard uses FastAPI with Jinja2Templates. base.html is the master template (95 lines) with a simple header (

Dark Factory

) inside a border-b container, followed by
with a content block. index.html extends base.html. The index route passes no context variables — just renders index.html. There is no /costs route yet; the task says the future costs.html will extend base.html. The approach: (1) Add a
element inside the existing
in base.html, with two links: Dashboard (/) and Costs (/costs). (2) Use Jinja2's request.url.path to determine the active page — this avoids needing any app.py changes or extra context variables, since Starlette's Request object (already available in all template renders) exposes the URL path. (3) Style with Tailwind classes consistent with the dark theme: bg-gray-900 background, gray-300/gray-400 text for inactive links, and a blue-500 accent (border-bottom or bg highlight) for the active link. (4) The nav is a simple horizontal bar with flex layout inside the header. (5) Update existing tests in test_app.py to verify nav presence, active state, and link hrefs. No new files needed — only modifications to base.html and test_app.py. index.html needs no changes since the nav is inherited from base.html.

steps.description: Write failing tests in test_app.py: TestNavBar class with tests for (1) nav element present in GET / response, (2) two nav links exist with correct hrefs (/ and /costs), (3) Dashboard link text present, (4) Costs link text present, (5) Dashboard link is marked active on / route, (6) nav uses dark theme classes (bg-gray-900 or gray-800 background).

Add nav bar to base.html inside the existing <header> element. Add a <nav> with flex layout containing two <a> links: Dashboard (href='/') and Costs (href='/costs'). Use request.url.path to conditionally apply active styling (border-b-2 border-blue-500 text-white) vs inactive styling (text-gray-400 hover:text-gray-200). Keep the 'Dark Factory' h1 as a brand element on the left, nav links on the right or beside it.

Verify all existing tests still pass after the base.html change. The nav bar addition may affect tests that count specific elements or check HTML structure (e.g., TestIndex, TestIdiomorphExtension, TestHtmxErrorHandling). Run full test suite to confirm no regressions.

Fix any test regressions caused by the nav bar addition. Likely none since existing tests check for content presence (not absence) and use string matching rather than strict DOM structure. If any tests assumed exact HTML structure of the header, update them to accommodate the nav.

design_decisions.decision: Use request.url.path for active page detection instead of a template variable
design_decisions.rationale: Starlette's Request object is already available in all TemplateResponse calls (passed as first arg). Using request.url.path avoids modifying app.py or adding context variables to every route handler. The Jinja2 expression {% if request.url.path == '/' %} is clean and self-contained.

design_decisions.decision: Place nav inside existing <header> element rather than as a separate element
design_decisions.rationale: The existing header has border-b border-gray-700 styling that serves as a visual separator. Adding nav links alongside the h1 in a flex container keeps the top bar cohesive and avoids adding extra vertical space. The header becomes a combined brand+nav bar.

design_decisions.decision: Use blue-500 border-bottom accent for active link instead of background color
design_decisions.rationale: A bottom border accent (border-b-2 border-blue-500) is a standard nav pattern that's subtle yet clear. It's consistent with the existing border-b on the header and avoids creating a heavy colored background that would clash with the gray-900 theme.

design_decisions.decision: Do not create costs.html or /costs route in this task
design_decisions.rationale: The task description says 'future costs.html will extend base.html'. This task only adds the nav bar. The /costs link will 404 until that page is implemented, which is acceptable for a nav bar that's being built ahead of the costs page.

design_decisions.decision: Add tests to existing test_app.py rather than creating a new test file
design_decisions.rationale: test_app.py already contains integration tests for the index page (TestIndex class) and base.html structure (TestIdiomorphExtension, TestHtmxErrorHandling). A new TestNavBar class in the same file follows the established pattern and shares the client fixture.

reuse.how: Reuse the existing client fixture that creates a TestClient for the FastAPI app. All new nav bar tests will use this fixture, same as existing TestIndex tests.
Modify the existing <header> to include nav links alongside the h1, using flex layout. No need for a separate nav container outside the header.

reuse.where: Available in all Jinja2 templates via the request object
reuse.how: Use {% if request.url.path == '/' %} to determine active nav link. No app.py changes needed since request is already passed to all TemplateResponse calls.

reuse.where: Existing templates use bg-gray-900, text-gray-100, border-gray-700, text-gray-400
reuse.how: Apply same color palette to nav: gray-400 for inactive links, white for active, blue-500 for accent border, gray-800 for hover states.
